text: The surgeon uses a sponge to keep the small bowel away from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, addressing challenges associated with high BMI patients.
A surgical sponge is used to keep the small bowel away from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, ensuring a clear surgical field.
The surgeon performs medial dissection to access the area beneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon performs medial dissection to access the area beneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery, carefully maneuvering around surrounding tissues.
The surgeon navigates through excess fatty tissue to locate the retrorectal plane, demonstrating perseverance in identifying the correct anatomical planes despite challenges posed by high BMI.
The surgeon navigates through excess fatty tissue to locate the retrorectal plane, gradually revealing the anatomical planes despite initial difficulty.
The surgeon struggles with retraction as the sigmoid colon flops down over the dissection area, obstructing the view and complicating the surgical maneuver.

The surgeon struggles with retraction as the sigmoid colon flops over the dissection area. Hand-over-hand technique is used to elevate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, while repositioning a sponge to maintain clear access.
The surgeon uses a hand-over-hand technique to elevate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, while repositioning a sponge to maintain separation of the small bowel from the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to isolate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and superior hemorrhoidal pedicle, maneuvering around the surrounding tissues to clearly define these vascular structures.
The surgeon attempts to isolate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and superior hemorrhoidal pedicle, maneuvering around the surrounding tissues to clearly delineate these vascular structures.
The surgeon navigates through an area with excess fatty tissue, searching for the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, which is obscured by the surrounding adipose tissue.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection through the excess fatty tissue, aiming to expose the superior hemorrhoidal vessel.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection, carefully approaching the area beneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery, navigating through the surrounding fatty tissue.
The surgeon switches to a vessel sealer due to the presence of excess fatty tissue, aiming to improve the dissection process.
The surgeon switches to a vessel sealer to improve hemostasis and security during dissection through the excess fatty tissue, replacing the scissors previously used.
The surgeon uses scissors to achieve secure hemostasis, ensuring that the tissue remains stable and blood flow is controlled.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect through the excess fatty tissue, aiming to isolate the superior hemorrhoidal pedicle.
The surgeon approaches the confluence of the superior hemorrhoidal artery and the left colic artery, navigating through the surrounding fatty tissue.
The surgeon approaches the confluence of the superior hemorrhoidal and left colic arteries, carefully identifying the left ureter before proceeding to take the mesenteric branches.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter before proceeding to take the mesenteric branches, ensuring the ureter is visually distinct from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies a structure ensconced beneath retroperitoneal fat, likely using visual cues to locate it amidst the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon identifies the junction of the superior hemorrhoidal and left colic arteries, visible beneath retroperitoneal fat. With the ureter safely out of the way, the surgeon proceeds to dissect through the pedicle at this location.
The surgeon visually identifies the junction of the superior hemorrhoidal artery with the left colic artery, emerging from the surrounding fatty tissue.
The surgeon proceeds to transect the pedicle at the identified location, ensuring the left ureter is safely out of the surgical field.
The ureter is visible again, identified amidst the surrounding tissue.
The ureter is visible again in the surgical field, appearing as a distinct tubular structure amidst the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to expose the descending colon and sigmoid colon, having previously mobilized around the splenic flexure.
The surgeon focuses on the descending colon and sigmoid colon during this portion of the dissection, revealing anatomical structures and surrounding tissue.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon by releasing the limited splenic flexure, allowing for greater maneuverability of the descending colon.
The surgical team prepares to initiate the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, focusing on the pelvic region.

The surgeon prepares for the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, suspending the uterus and right ovary with sutures to clear the surgical field. The right mesorectal excision dissection is then performed.
The surgeon suspends the uterus and right ovary with sutures to clear the surgical field for better access.
The surgeon performs a dissection of the right mesorectal excision, carefully separating the mesorectal tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection in the posterior plane, encountering an excess of fatty tissue that needs to be carefully navigated.
The surgeon encounters an excess of fatty tissue within the dissection plane during the low anterior resection procedure.
The surgeon proceeds with dissection on the left side, continuing the total mesorectal excision (TME) approach.
The surgeon moves down the left side, preparing to dissect across the anterior portion. The dissection is angled upwards at thirty degrees, allowing access to the rectovaginal septum.
The surgeon prepares to dissect across the anterior portion, adjusting the angle to approximately thirty degrees upward.
The surgeon navigates into the rectovaginal septum, focusing on the anatomical space between the rectum and vagina.
The surgeon continues to work circumferentially around the rectal area, navigating through tissue planes where dissection appears easier.

The surgeon continues the circumferential dissection around the rectum, navigating through the tissue planes to reach the desired level for total mesorectal excision (TME). The approach involves working around challenging areas to ensure complete dissection.
The surgeon continues circumferential dissection, navigating around the rectal tissue in a challenging case, aiming to reach the desired anatomical level.
The surgeon checks the dissection site to ensure it is positioned beneath the tumor, verifying the accuracy of the surgical approach.

The surgeon checks the location of the tumor to ensure the dissection is beneath it. The colonic and rectal mesentery are thinned using a vessel sealer. A 45-load stapler is positioned in the deep pelvis, and two loads are fired to transect the rectum just above the pelvic diaphragm.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to thin out the colonic or rectal mesentery before deploying a 45-load stapler. The stapler is maneuvered into the deep pelvis, and two loads are used to transect the rectum just above the pelvic diaphragm.

The surgeon plans a stapled anastomosis due to the low position of the anastomosis site, with approximately three to four centimeters of rectum beneath it. The proximal colon mesentery is being taken in preparation for the anastomosis.
The surgeon prepares to perform a stapled anastomosis in the deep pelvis, positioning the stapler to connect the colonic and rectal segments.
The surgeon is dissecting the proximal colon mesentery, separating it from surrounding tissues in preparation for the anastomosis.
The surgeon advances the dissection up to the colon margin, preparing the tissue for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon has completed the mobilization of the splenic flexure, which involves freeing the colon from its attachments near the spleen.
The surgeon checks the perfusion of the mobilized splenic flexure using ICG mode, ensuring adequate blood flow to the tissue.
The surgeon checks the perfusion of the tissue using indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence imaging mode, observing the vascular supply and perfusion quality.
The surgeon marks the charred portion of the tissue to ensure its removal during exteriorization.
The surgeon marks the charred portion of the tissue to ensure its removal upon exteriorization. The stapled end of the colon is visible as it is exteriorized through a Pfannenstiel incision.
The surgeon displays the stapled end of the bowel, indicating the completion of the anastomosis.
The surgeon exteriorizes the bowel through a fan and steel incision, bringing the stapled end into view.
The surgeon places the anvil into the bowel segment and prepares to perform the anastomosis.

The surgeon places the anvil in the proximal colon and positions the EEA stapler for the anastomosis. The surgeon manually mates the two ends of the colon, releases the anvil, and then moves between the patient's legs to tighten the EEA stapler, completing the anastomosis.
The surgeon places the anvil in position, preparing for the anastomosis without assistance from below.
The surgeon places the EEA (end-to-end anastomosis) stapler in position, preparing for the anastomosis procedure.
The surgeon aligns and connects the two ends of the bowel to complete the anastomosis.
The surgeon releases the anvil, which is positioned for the anastomosis during the low anterior resection procedure.
The surgeon positions themselves between the patient's legs to tighten the EEA (end-to-end anastomosis) stapler, ensuring proper alignment and tension for the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis, joining the two ends of the bowel using the EEA stapler.

The surgeon inspects the anastomosis site using a rigid scope, confirming there is no bleeding. The anastomosis is positioned approximately three centimeters from the anal verge. A leak test is performed, showing no leakage and a widely patent connection.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis site, checking for any visible signs of leakage or fluid escape.
The surgeon uses a rigid scope to inspect the surgical site, confirming the absence of bleeding.
The surgeon positions the anastomosis approximately three centimeters from the anal verge, ensuring proper alignment for the connection.
The surgeon inspects the anastomosis site using a rigid scope, confirming visually that there is no leakage present.
